Teaching
Courses Cumulative average for all course evaluations: 4.8/5
___________________Rowan University___________________
Seminar in Communication Studies: “This writing intensive course provides a seminar experience in areas of
communication that are not part of the regular course offerings. Examples
of potential topics include Friendship, Rhetoric of Music, Romantic
Relationships, and Presidential Campaigns.” Theme: Rhetoric of Science.
Instructor of Record: Spring 2020
Public Speaking: “This course trains students in the fundamentals of public speaking,
including study and practice of speech preparation and speech delivery.
The goal is to enable the student to participate effectively in oral
communication, as a student, professionally and as a citizen.”
Instructor of Record: Winter 2020
Miles C. Coleman
Rhetoric of Science, Technology, and Medicine – Communication Ethics – Computational Media
14 March 2020
Rhetoric of Science, Technology
and, Medicine: “Students are introduced to key thinkers and theories in the area of
rhetoric of science, technology, and medicine (RSTM). Students marshal
their learning to study a chosen RSTM topic/problem and create messaging
to solve that problem. Identity and ideology in scientific, technological, and
medical discourses are highlighted throughout.”
Instructor of Record: Fall 2019
Rhetorical Criticism: “This course surveys ancient to modern theories of speech criticism to
develop defensible criteria for evaluating speeches, social movements, and
non-oratorical events. Students study and evaluate past and present public
speeches by applying various rhetorical standards.”
Instructor of Record: Spring 2020; Fall 2019

Speaking in the Organization: “Contemporary communication techniques for use in business and
industry. Principles and practice of oral reporting, persuasive speaking,
conference participation, and interpersonal interaction.”

Teaching Assistant: Spring 2009; Spring 2008
Introduction to Communication: Instructor of Record: Fall 2010
Course in Development
Introduction to Health and Science
Communication: “Introduction to health and science communication. Students learn about
the ways in which people make sense of health and illness, environmental
risk, and science policy.”
Expected Completion: Fall 2020
Guest Lectures/Community Workshops
“AIDS Denialism on the Internet,” invited colloquium presented to 15 faculty and undergraduate students
at Rowan University as part of the Communication Studies Colloquium Series, March 2020.
“Communication Ethics and Vaccine Denialism,” invited lecture presented to 10 doctoral medical students
at Rowan University, School of Osteopathic Medicine, March 2020.
“Rhetoric, Ethics, and Public Vaccine Science,” invited lecture presented to 80 doctoral nursing students at
Seattle University, June 2019.
“Sonic Rhetorics: A Brief Exercise for Thinking about, and Making, Sound,” invited workshop presented/run
for 15 high school students for the Seattle Ethnic Heritage Council, August 2018.
“Human Machine Interfaces and the Future of Interaction,” invited panel presentation for a 100-person
general audience for the MIT Enterprise Northwest, May 2018. Covered in GeekWire magazine:
https://www.geekwire.com/2018/worried-fake-news-get-ready-coming-rise-fake-humans/.
“Integrating Slack(ing) for Great Classroom Communication,” invited presentation given to a 10-faculty
audience at the Center for Digital Learning and Innovation, November 2016.
“Communication Matters: Mastering Public Speaking as a Researcher,” invited presentation given to 100
graduate students and faculty for the Institute of Translational Health Sciences, University of
Washington, May 2016.

“Narrative and Electronic Media in Business: When Your Story Clashes With the Stories of

“Darwin and His Story: The Narrative Paradigm and Science,” invited lecture, presented to a

“Style as Argument: Composure and the Nondiscursive in Professional Settings,” invited

Other/Creative Work
“sounds of human impact”—a dynamic web application, which sources the Audubon Society’s
archive of bird songs, and an algorithm that sonically represents the differences in species
displacement caused by anthropocentric climate change in New Jersey between 1970 and
2020. The project is being developed in collaboration with Dr. Charles Schutte
(Environmental Science, Rowan University), and is planned to be a physical installation at
Rowan University. Access to a working prototype:
https://milesccoleman.com/soundsofhumanimpact/.
“feels_bot”—an interactive exhibit constructed from JavaScript and utilizes artificial intelligence and
crowdsourced .gifs to “catch feels” from users. Physically installed as part of the “Pull Request” art
exhibition in the Vachon Gallery at Seattle University Sept.-Oct. 2018. https://www.seattleu.
edu/artsci/about/news/news-2018/pull-request-collaborative-code-art.html. Access to an online
version of the exhibit: https://milesccoleman.weebly.com/ feels_bot.html.
Miles C. Coleman
Rhetoric of Science, Technology, and Medicine – Communication Ethics – Computational Media
14 March 2020
“sylla_bot”—a Python-based chatbot that uses machine learning to learn, and answer questions about, a
course syllabus. Access to the project page: https://milesccoleman.com/sylla_bot/.
